questions.prompt: What does the cost-of-defect curve say?
options: All defects cost the same to fix
The later a defect is caught, the more expensive it is to fix
Defects found in production are cheapest
Cost depends only on severity, not timing
questions.explanation: Cost rises sharply with each phase a defect survives — roughly an order of magnitude per phase.

options: Do all testing right before release
Move testing activities earlier in the lifecycle
Let developers skip testing
Shift bugs to the next sprint
questions.explanation: Shifting left catches defects when they are cheapest — in requirements, design and development.

questions.prompt: A story is marked done but has no acceptance criteria. The good QA move is:
options: Pass it — no criteria means nothing to fail
Raise it in refinement/planning and get testable acceptance criteria defined
Silently write your own criteria and tell no one
Skip the story entirely
questions.explanation: Untestable stories are a process gap; surface it early so the team agrees on what 'done' means.

questions.prompt: A developer says "it's a tiny change, no need to test." Best response?
options: Agree — small changes are always safe
Assess the risk; even small changes can break things, so at least smoke-test the impact area
Refuse to speak to the developer
Block the entire release on principle
questions.explanation: Risk, not size, drives testing. Small changes cause real regressions; gauge impact and test accordingly.

questions.prompt: In Scrum, where does QA belong?
options: Only in a separate testing phase after the sprint
Across the ceremonies — refinement, planning, the sprint, review and retro
Only in the retrospective
QA has no role in Scrum
questions.explanation: Agile has no late testing phase to hide in; QA contributes throughout the sprint and its ceremonies.
